ocar.opportunity: Whole-brain connectomics in a vertebrate had not been achieved; the larval zebrafish, with its ~100,000-neuron brain and optical transparency, offered the first tractable vertebrate target for complete brain-level EM imaging.
ocar.challenge: Acquiring serial-section EM data for an entire vertebrate brain volume — orders of magnitude larger than invertebrate precedents — requires handling thousands of ultrathin sections without loss or damage and stitching the resulting terabytes of imaging data.
ocar.action: The authors collected serial-section transmission EM data spanning the entire larval zebrafish brain, developing protocols for section collection at scale and computational pipelines for image stitching, alignment, and initial volume assembly.
ocar.resolution: The study produced the first whole-brain serial-section EM volume of a vertebrate, providing a resource enabling study of circuit connectivity at synaptic resolution across the entire nervous system. Initial analyses demonstrated the feasibility of tracing long-range axonal projections across brain regions.
ocar.future_work: Completing dense reconstruction across the entire volume will require automated segmentation and proofreading at a scale not yet achieved; targeted circuit analyses in specific brain regions are more immediately tractable.

summaries.beginner: Scientists imaged an entire zebrafish larval brain with a powerful electron microscope — the first time this was done for any vertebrate. The result is a massive three-dimensional dataset where individual nerve cells and their connections can be traced throughout the whole brain.
summaries.intermediate: This landmark study produced the first whole-brain serial-section EM volume of a vertebrate — the larval zebrafish — enabling synapse-resolution circuit analysis across all brain regions. Scalable section collection and alignment pipelines were developed, demonstrating that complete vertebrate brain EM is achievable. The dataset underpins follow-up circuit studies requiring long-range axonal tracing.
summaries.advanced: This paper describes the acquisition and assembly of a whole-brain serial-section TEM volume of larval zebrafish (~1,000 µm anterior-posterior), the first such dataset for any vertebrate. Key technical contributions include section handling at scale and computational stitching/alignment methods. The dataset is a resource enabling targeted circuit reconstruction but faces challenges of dense reconstruction: full segmentation and proofreading of ~100,000 neurons will require automated tools far beyond current capacity. Z-resolution is limited by section thickness (~40 nm), meaning thin processes can be missed.

discussion_prompts: What are the unique biological advantages of larval zebrafish over Drosophila as a model for whole-brain connectomics, and what new scientific questions become tractable with a vertebrate whole-brain EM volume?
How does the challenge of reconstructing long-range axonal projections in a vertebrate brain differ from circuit reconstruction in compact invertebrate ganglia?
What automated segmentation and annotation strategies would be most critical for enabling dense circuit reconstruction in this whole-brain dataset?
